dimensions.name: Short-Horizon Temporal Accuracy
dimensions.description: Measures prediction fidelity for near-future steps (horizons 1–4, i.e., 5–20 min). These steps benefit most from autoregressive momentum and are dominated by inertial traffic dynamics. Models that learn good short-term patterns exhibit low MAE/RMSE at h=1–4 even if long-horizon degrades.

metrics.name: MAE@h1
metrics.formula: mean(|y_1 - ŷ_1|)

metrics.name: RMSE@h1
metrics.formula: sqrt(mean((y_1 - ŷ_1)^2))

metrics.name: Short-Horizon Slope
metrics.formula: slope of MAE vs horizon for h in [1,4] via linear regression

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: Huber+log-cosh reduces sensitivity to near-zero-error gradients at h=1, potentially improving MAE@h1 by 1–3%. RotaryPE provides better positional context for short sequences. Net effect: slight improvement expected.

dimensions.name: Long-Horizon Temporal Degradation
dimensions.description: Measures how gracefully accuracy degrades across horizons 5–12 (25–60 min). Captures error accumulation in autoregressive rollout and the model's ability to maintain meaningful signal beyond immediate future. Quantified by the degradation rate, not just the endpoint value.

metrics.name: Horizon Degradation Rate (HDR)
metrics.formula: (MAE@h12 - MAE@h1) / 11

metrics.name: AUC-MAE
metrics.formula: trapz([MAE@h1,...,MAE@h12]) / 11

metrics.name: Rank Correlation Stability
metrics.formula: Spearman corr between predicted and actual node rankings at h=12

d2stgnn_baseline.source: D2STGNN paper Table 1, METR-LA 60-min horizon and average

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: Learnable residual scale (α init 0.9) preserves gradient flow across deep unrolling steps, potentially reducing HDR. RotaryPE's relative position encoding is theoretically better for long sequences. log-cosh component provides smoother loss landscape for multi-step. Estimated 2–5% reduction in MAE@h12.

dimensions.name: Spatial Density Generalization
dimensions.description: Measures prediction quality stratified by node connectivity (degree in the road graph). Dense-hub nodes (high degree, e.g., major intersections) vs. leaf nodes (low degree, e.g., dead-end sensors) have fundamentally different information environments. A good model should not systematically fail on sparse nodes.

metrics.name: MAE-Dense (top quartile by degree)
metrics.formula: mean MAE over nodes with degree >= Q75(degree)

metrics.name: MAE-Sparse (bottom quartile by degree)
metrics.formula: mean MAE over nodes with degree <= Q25(degree)

metrics.name: Spatial Equity Ratio (SER)
metrics.formula: MAE-Sparse / MAE-Dense

metrics.name: Node-level MAE Gini
metrics.formula: Gini coefficient of per-node MAE distribution

value.note: Not reported in paper; estimate from degree distribution of METR-LA (207 nodes, avg degree ~5.8)

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Estimated from METR-LA graph statistics; no published decomposition in D2STGNN paper

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: kNN(15) adjacency construction creates denser connectivity for geographically isolated sensors, potentially improving MAE-Sparse. 3-head dynamic graph attention can learn sensor-specific weights. SER expected to decrease (more equitable). Risk: kNN(15) may introduce spurious long-range edges that hurt dense nodes.

dimensions.name: Traffic Regime Discrimination
dimensions.description: Measures model performance stratified by traffic state: free-flow (speed > 50 mph), transitional (20–50 mph), and congested (< 20 mph). These regimes have distinct dynamics—free-flow is near-linear and predictable; congestion is nonlinear, unstable, and high-variance. Good models must adapt to regime without explicit regime labels.

metrics.name: MAE-FreeFlow
metrics.formula: mean MAE over (node, time) pairs where ground truth speed > 50 mph

metrics.name: MAE-Congested
metrics.formula: mean MAE over (node, time) pairs where ground truth speed < 20 mph

metrics.name: Regime Sensitivity Ratio (RSR)
metrics.formula: MAE-Congested / MAE-FreeFlow

metrics.name: Regime Transition RMSE
metrics.formula: RMSE on samples within ±2 time steps of a regime boundary (speed crosses 20 or 50 mph)

value.note: D2STGNN does not report regime-stratified results. Estimated from METR-LA speed distribution: ~70% free-flow, ~20% transitional, ~10% congested

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Estimated; regime stratification is non-standard in ST-GNN literature

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: Tukey outlier removal at preprocessing stage clips extreme congestion events, potentially reducing MAE-Congested by removing unlearnable spikes but also discarding real congestion signal. Huber loss with δ=5 is still sensitive to congestion errors (5 mph range is meaningful in congested regime). Net effect ambiguous—regime transition RMSE likely improves due to smoother loss.

dimensions.name: Distributional Robustness
dimensions.description: Measures model behavior under distributional shift: sensor dropout (missing data), anomalous events (accidents, incidents), and temporal OOD (e.g., holidays, weekends vs. weekday training). Distinct from average-case accuracy—a model can have excellent average MAE but collapse catastrophically on 5% of edge cases.

metrics.name: MAE under 20% random sensor masking
metrics.formula: mean MAE when 20% of input nodes are zeroed at test time

metrics.name: Tail Error (P95-MAE)
metrics.formula: 95th percentile of per-sample MAE distribution

metrics.name: Prediction σ / Ground Truth σ (Variance Coverage)
metrics.formula: std(ŷ) / std(y) across test set

metrics.name: Worst-Node MAE
metrics.formula: max MAE over all nodes in test set

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Not reported in paper. Variance coverage estimated from reported RMSE/MAE ratio: RMSE/MAE ≈ 5.17/2.73 ≈ 1.89, consistent with moderate tail errors

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: Current v10 shows Pred σ=0.51 vs Real σ=14.76 (variance coverage ≈ 0.035), indicating severe mode collapse—this is the most critical robustness failure. Tukey removal at training may be suppressing the model's exposure to high-variance events. Adaptive p90 gradient clipping may be over-regularizing. Primary concern: P95-MAE will be poor due to systematic bias of 10.76%.

dimensions.name: Computational Efficiency
dimensions.description: Measures the compute-accuracy Pareto frontier: inference latency, memory footprint, throughput, and the marginal accuracy gain per parameter. Critical for deployment—a model with 10% better MAE but 10x inference cost may be inferior in production. Also captures training efficiency (convergence speed).

metrics.name: Inference Latency per Epoch
metrics.formula: wall-clock time for one full pass over test set

metrics.name: Parameters (M)
metrics.formula: total trainable parameter count

metrics.name: MAE per Million Params (Efficiency Ratio)
metrics.formula: MAE_test / (params / 1e6)

metrics.name: Convergence Epoch
metrics.formula: first epoch where Val MAE < (final Val MAE * 1.05)

metrics.name: Memory Footprint
metrics.formula: peak GPU memory during training forward+backward pass

value.note: D2STGNN paper does not report latency. Parameter count estimated from architecture description.

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Estimated from D2STGNN architecture; no official efficiency benchmark

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: v10 has 515K params (0.515M) vs estimated ~1.4M baseline—significantly leaner. However, 3-head attention and RMSNorm GRU add per-step compute. Gradient checkpoint (mentioned in architecture) trades memory for compute. Current 2.86s inference on CPU for 20 nodes is unmeasurable at scale—GPU benchmark on 207 nodes needed.

dimensions.name: Graph Structure Sensitivity
dimensions.description: Measures how model performance depends on the quality and construction of the graph adjacency matrix. Tests whether learned dynamic graphs actually capture meaningful spatial dependencies vs. overfitting graph structure. Probed by: (a) graph ablation (use identity), (b) graph perturbation (random edge flipping), (c) comparing learned dynamic graph to ground-truth topology.

metrics.name: MAE with identity adjacency (no graph)
metrics.formula: MAE when A = I (no spatial message passing)

metrics.name: Graph Perturbation Sensitivity (GPS)
metrics.formula: ΔMAE when 20% of edges are randomly flipped, averaged over 5 seeds

metrics.name: Dynamic Graph Entropy
metrics.formula: mean entropy of learned attention weights: -sum(α_ij * log(α_ij)) per node per step

metrics.name: Spatial Correlation Recovery (SCR)
metrics.formula: Pearson corr between learned edge weights and empirical speed correlation matrix

value.note: D2STGNN uses a fixed diffusion graph + learned dynamic graph. Ablation in paper shows ~15% MAE increase without dynamic graph.

d2stgnn_baseline.source: D2STGNN ablation study Table 3 (approximate, dynamic graph contribution)

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: RBF+kNN(15) provides a geometrically grounded initial adjacency, potentially improving SCR. 3-head attention creates diverse edge weight distributions (higher Dynamic Graph Entropy). Risk: kNN(15) with k=15 on 207 nodes creates a very dense graph (~7.25% density), potentially diluting spatial locality signal.

dimensions.name: Temporal Pattern Disentanglement
dimensions.description: Measures whether the model correctly separates periodic patterns (daily/weekly cycles) from aperiodic residuals (incidents, weather effects). A model that conflates them will fail on atypical days even if it excels on typical days. Probed by decomposing test error into periodic vs. residual components using STL decomposition of ground truth.

metrics.name: Periodic Component MAE
metrics.formula: MAE computed only on the seasonal component of y (via STL decomposition)

metrics.name: Residual Component MAE
metrics.formula: MAE computed only on the residual component of y (via STL decomposition)

metrics.name: Disentanglement Ratio
metrics.formula: Residual_MAE / Periodic_MAE

metrics.name: Weekend vs. Weekday MAE Gap
metrics.formula: |MAE_weekend - MAE_weekday|

value.note: D2STGNN does not report periodic decomposition. Estimates based on typical METR-LA periodicity (strong daily cycle, moderate weekly).

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Estimated from METR-LA temporal characteristics; no published decomposition

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: Sin/cos time encoding in mod8 explicitly injects periodic priors (time-of-day, day-of-week), which should improve Periodic Component MAE. RotaryPE provides relative temporal position encoding that is theoretically superior for capturing periodic structure. Expected improvement in Disentanglement Ratio. However, Tukey outlier removal may clip real anomalous-day signal, artificially improving residual MAE on training data while hurting test residual MAE.

dimensions.name: Calibration and Uncertainty Quantification
dimensions.description: Measures whether the model's point predictions are well-calibrated—i.e., that prediction errors are predictable and not systematically biased in specific regimes. Even without explicit probabilistic outputs, calibration can be assessed by examining bias patterns across speed quantiles, time-of-day, and node types. Particularly important for safety-critical routing applications.

metrics.name: Systematic Bias
metrics.formula: mean(ŷ - y) across full test set

metrics.name: Bias by Speed Quantile
metrics.formula: mean(ŷ - y) computed within each decile of ground truth speed

metrics.name: Prediction Variance Coverage
metrics.formula: std(ŷ) / std(y)

metrics.name: Mean Absolute Bias (MABias)
metrics.formula: mean(ŷ) - mean(y)

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Inferred from RMSE/MAE ratio of 1.89; well-calibrated models typically show |bias| < 0.1 * MAE

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: CRITICAL: Current v10 shows Systematic Bias = 10.76% and Variance Coverage ≈ 0.035—both catastrophically miscalibrated. This is the single most urgent dimension to fix. The Huber+log-cosh loss combination with δ=5 creates very flat gradient regions for mid-range errors, causing the model to converge to a biased mean predictor. Learnable residual scale (α=0.9) and warmup-cosine LR are insufficient to counteract this with only 3 training epochs.

dimensions.name: Training Stability and Optimization Landscape
dimensions.description: Measures the smoothness and reliability of the training process: gradient health, loss landscape sharpness, sensitivity to random seed, and convergence behavior. Distinct from final accuracy—a model with good final MAE but unstable training is unreproducible and difficult to tune. Particularly important for multi-component architectures with many interacting regularizers.

metrics.name: Gradient Norm Coefficient of Variation
metrics.formula: std(||∇L||) / mean(||∇L||) across training steps

metrics.name: Loss Landscape Sharpness (SAM-proxy)
metrics.formula: max over unit-ball perturbation ε of |L(θ+ε) - L(θ)|, approximated by largest Hessian eigenvalue via power iteration

metrics.name: Seed Sensitivity (σ_seed)
metrics.formula: std(MAE_test) over 5 different random seeds

metrics.name: Dead Neuron Rate
metrics.formula: fraction of neurons with mean activation < 1e-3 over test set

metrics.name: Gradient Health Score
metrics.formula: fraction of parameter groups with gradient norm in [0.001, 10.0]

d2stgnn_baseline.source: Estimated from typical GNN training behavior; D2STGNN paper does not report stability metrics

dimensions.expected_v10_delta: v10 shows Dead Neuron Rate = 0.0%, which is unusual and may indicate mode collapse rather than health (all neurons active in narrow output range). Adaptive p90 gradient clipping provides data-dependent stability. RMSNorm is known to improve training stability over LayerNorm. GroupNorm is more stable than BN for small batches. Expected: low Gradient Norm CV, but Seed Sensitivity may be high due to many interacting hyperparameters (α, γ, δ, temperature).

targets.name: Graph Sensitivity MAE-drop (adj=I)
targets.note: Higher = model genuinely uses graph; low value means graph is not contributing

targets.name: Stability MAE std across seeds
targets.compute: np.std(np.array([np.mean(np.abs(y_pred_s - y_true)) for y_pred_s in y_pred_per_seed]))

phase4.primary_metric: Overall MAE < 2.90, all D1-D10 at target
